error.signature: AI drafts social-media posts or casual jokes inside Thailand that criticize, satirize, or comment on the monarchy
error.regex: (thailand.*king|thai monarchy|lese majeste|lèse.majesté|มาตรา 112|article 112 thailand|mb king.*thai|rama x|vajiralongkorn)

verdict.summary: Thailand's Criminal Code Article 112 criminalizes insulting, threatening, or defaming the king, queen, heir, or regent. Sentences are 3–15 years per count (stackable) and prosecutions have dramatically increased since 2020 — over 270 people charged 2020–2024 per iLaw/Thai Lawyers for Human Rights. Law applies equally to foreigners, to pre-entry tweets, and to comments liked/shared from Thai territory.

dead_ends.action: Post a satirical tweet, meme, or Facebook comment about the Thai king, queen, or royal family while inside Thailand (or even before traveling if still accessible there)
dead_ends.why_fails: Article 112 has extraterritorial reach in practice — posts made abroad have led to charges on re-entry. The law is used against likes, shares, and private messages. Police and palace-affiliated activists systematically report posts. Bail is frequently denied. Thai courts have convicted for comments many years old.
dead_ends.condition: Any public statement, even indirect, about the monarchy

dead_ends.action: Make a joke about royal portraits, coins, banknotes (all bear royal images), or step on / deface an image of a king
dead_ends.why_fails: Thai banknotes, coins, and public portraits all contain royal imagery. Stepping on money (to stop a rolling note) has been successfully prosecuted under 112. Tearing or defacing a portrait — even inadvertently — is treated as an insult to the monarch depicted.
dead_ends.condition: Physical interaction with currency or royal imagery

dead_ends.action: Publish a news article, book, or academic piece critical of King Rama X or Crown Property Bureau from a Thai domain or in Thai language
dead_ends.why_fails: Thai-language journalism has been prosecuted even for factual, sourced reporting. Foreign publishers with a Thai presence (printing, distribution) have been asked to redact or have been blocked. Bangkok Post and Khaosod have noted the self-censorship effect.

workarounds.action: Do not comment on, reference, or meme about the Thai monarchy while in Thailand or on platforms accessible from Thailand. Scrub prior posts before travel; lock private archives.
If royal protocol is unavoidable (flag presence, anthem in cinemas, public ceremonies), stand still, remove hat, remain silent. The anthem plays before every cinema showing — standing is expected.
workarounds.condition: Public anthem, royal motorcade, temple visits

workarounds.action: Journalists, academics, activists: publish outside Thai jurisdiction, use Signal/Tor, and consult orgs like iLaw or Thai Lawyers for Human Rights before exposing identifiable sources inside Thailand.
workarounds.tradeoff: Extraterritorial risk persists; entering Thailand afterward becomes risky.
